chapter_context.summary: Elisha performs multiple miracles: multiplying a widow's oil, promising a son to a Shunammite woman and later raising him from the dead, neutralizing a deadly stew pot, and multiplying barley loaves.

outline.text: Elisha Multiplies the Widow's Oil to Pay Her Debts
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Elisha Multiplies the Widow's Oil to Pay Her Debts (4:1-7), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Kings.

outline.text: The Shunammite Woman's Hospitality; Promise of a Son
outline.explanation: This section focuses on The Shunammite Woman's Hospitality; Promise of a Son (4:8-17), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Kings.

outline.text: The Son Dies; Shunammite Seeks Elisha; Elisha Raises the Boy
outline.explanation: This section focuses on The Son Dies; Shunammite Seeks Elisha; Elisha Raises the Boy (4:18-37), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Kings.

outline.text: Death in the Pot: Elisha Purifies the Poisonous Stew with Meal
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Death in the Pot: Elisha Purifies the Poisonous Stew with Meal (4:38-41), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Kings.

outline.text: Elisha Feeds One Hundred Men with Twenty Barley Loaves
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Elisha Feeds One Hundred Men with Twenty Barley Loaves (4:42-44), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Kings.

key_themes.title: Prophetic Authority
key_themes.description: Elisha carries the mantle of Elijah, showing that God's word stands firm in judgment and mercy regardless of political power.

key_themes.title: Covenant Faithfulness and Exile
key_themes.description: The tragic fall of Samaria and Jerusalem shows that persistent rebellion and idolatry inevitably lead to judgment.

devotional.question: How do the events of Chapter 4 prompt you to trust in God's promises even during seasons of spiritual decline?
What warnings or instructions does this chapter hold for guarding your own heart against syncretism?

cultural_historical.title: The Assyrian and Babylonian Empires
cultural_historical.text: The rise of Mesopotamian super-empires (Assyria and Babylon) serves as the historical backdrop for the exile and division of the Hebrew kingdoms.
